headline: The Malay grievance isn't about race. It's about policy capture.
context: Across published findings, three measurable leaks in Bumiputera redistribution share one pattern: programmes built for B40 Malays reach middlemen, absentee board appointees, and T20 households more than the intended beneficiaries.

cards.big: Bumiputera procurement was built for B40 Malays. The data shows it mostly reaches middlemen and T20 boards.
cards.sub: Three measurable leaks — RM12B in contract rent-seeking, RM680M in absentee board fees, race-blanket discounts that ignore income — share one pattern.

cards.big: RM12 billion a year leaks from the Bumiputera contract quota to Ali Baba middlemen — 27% of the RM45 billion Bumi allocation.
cards.sub: Genuine Bumiputera contractors lose to commission-seekers. MACC prosecuted 42 cases in 2025 against 8,000-12,000 estimated arrangements.

cards.big: 342 political appointees earn RM680 million from GLC boards for ~4 hours of monthly meetings each — RM1.99 million average per appointee.
cards.sub: 87% have no industry expertise in the GLCs they oversee. Board fees grew 34% since 2022 while worker wages in those same GLCs grew 4.8%.

cards.big: Intra-Bumiputera wealth inequality (Gini 0.39) is greater than inter-ethnic inequality (Gini 0.34) — the bigger gap is within, not between.
cards.sub: Race-based policy addresses the smaller distribution. The Bumiputera property discount is not means-tested — a T20 Bumi household gets the same as a B40.

cards.big: The Malay grievance isn't that Malays lose to other races. It's that programmes built for B40 Malays are captured by Malay elites.
Means-test the discounts. Hire GLC boards on merit. Track contracts by actual workforce, not ownership. Each reform is pro-Malay — the rent-seekers are not the community.
